docs.title: UPSCb-common introduction
docs.text: Here you will find the most basic information about the bio-info facility's infrastructure and how to properly use it to ensure minimum disruptions and maximum productivity. Please read this guide to gain a basic idea of what resources are at your disposal and how to use them properly. If and when you come across an unfamiliar term, please visit the Frequently used terms/resources section.

docs.title: Infrastructure
docs.text: At UPSCb we have our own servers for both computation and storage. We also use regional and national resources.

docs.title: Local
docs.text: Our on-site machines are used for both computation and storage. A few names you will come across frequently are riboexplorer, aspseq and microasp. All of these machines are accessed via ssh(more information on accessing these can be found below).

docs.title: National/Regional
docs.text: High Performance Computing Center North HPC2N offers an Open On Demand system to request and use their resources. We will help you gain access to this resource and provide intial training to use it. There are several such computing centers in Sweden operating under the National Academic Infrastructure for Super­computing in Sweden NAISS umbrella. Access to these can be discussed according to the computational needs of individual projects.

docs.title: Bash vs R
docs.text: On your data analysis journey with us at UPSCb you will primarily use bash and R. In order for things to go smoothly, you need to know what tools you can use for a given task.

docs.title: Bash:
docs.text: Generally, you will use bash (scripts that end with .sh) to do data preprocessing and housekeeping of your data. Bash scripts are run using a terminal (Shell in windows, terminal in macOS) which is a text based user interface that allows you to interact with a computer (in our case a server). In order to maintain sanity and avoid CPU overload we use a queuing system called SLURM to submit ‘jobs’ (i.e running a script).

docs.title: R:
docs.text: We have a repository of pre-written scripts as well as templates that you can use to write your own scripts for both bash and R. These are available at

docs.title: Frequently used terms/resources
docs.text:
1. Git:
  2. In technical terms: 'A version control system (VCS) used to track changes to your source code. You can learn more about Git here.

  3. Learn about Git in not-so tech terms: here

  4. Github: Website that hosts public and private code documented using Git.

  5. R and R-studio: One of the most important tools that is used frequently in UPSCb data anlaysis pipelines. If you are not familiar with R, please go through these resources: swirl, discovR

  6. SLURM : It is a queueing system meant to ensure the concurrent and fair use of a compute cluster. You can learn more about SLURM and SLURM commands like sbatch and squeue here

  7. Video: Here is a video explaining how we use Git and SLURM at UPSCb.

  8. Containers: As the name suggests, containers allow code for target tools/applications to be packaged and isolated (in other words: contained) alongwith their dependecies. In practice, it makes it easy for different users to run the tools consistently regardless of their run environment. A video explaination can be found here

  9. More information about the containers and their usage can be found here

docs.title: CITATION
docs.text: Please use to cite the use of this repos :-)

docs.title: IMPORTANT NOTICE
docs.text: 2022/12/06 I found a serious bug in the DE template! I have corrected the DE template: https://github.com/UPSCb/UPSCb-common/tree/master/templates/R, but please check out your analysis done since 2021/03/05. Sadly, it means that any analysis needs redoing! :disappointed: :disappointed: :disappointed: The issue is that the template used the lfcThreshold when retrieving the DE results (using the DESeq2 results function). What this does, unlike the alpha parameter that sets the FDR threshold to return results, is to change the test that is being done. Instead of comparing for a difference in expression of 0, it tests for a difference in expression at the selected value (+0.5 by default). Results are likely to be quite drastically different!

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
There are three important directories:

|- pipeline
|- src
  |- bash
  |- R
|- templates
  |- R
  1. pipeline is a directory that contains scripts written (mostly) in bash to be copied into your project and used through a SLURM queueing system.
  2. src (source) contains a number of subdirectories sorted by programming language holding a number of utilities
  3. templates contains a number of subdirectories sorted by programming language holding a number of templates to be re-used. Most notably it contains examples of data analysis in R (e.g. BiologicalQA.R, DifferentialExpression.R, etc.) and some bash examples to submit jobs to the queue.

docs.title: Templates
docs.text:
Before re-inventing the wheel, check the templates directory! A number of useful templates are available there:

  1. R/empty.R to initiate an R script with the Rmd header and session info blocks.
  2. R/BiologicalQA.R to do the initial Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) of your RNA-Seq data
  3. R/DifferentialExpression.R to perform a Differential Expression (DE) of your data (follows the previous one)
  4. bash/runTemplate.sh to prototype script to be run on an HPC (High Perf. Compute) using SLURM (a job manager)

docs.text:

docs.title: Contact us
docs.text: For UPSC members, ask us to be added to our Slack channel as well as mailing list. These are the two channels we use to communicate about server updates and downtime (as well as other technical issues), but also those we use to discuss projects, provide support, etc.

docs.title: Useful bash one-liners
docs.text: Edit this file to add oneliners (you can cheat with ; ) you find useful. If there is no appropriate sub-heading, feel free to create one.

docs.title: Table of contents
docs.text:
1. SLURM related
    • Cancel all SLURM jobs that you have currently running
    • Delete all SLURM logs in the current directory (and all sub-directories)
  2. File and folder management
    • Find all human-readable files with pattern in text
  3. Miscellanea
  4. Docker
  5. RNA-Seq Preprocessing

docs.title: Cancel all SLURM jobs that you have currently running
docs.text: squeue -h -u $USER | awk '{ print $1 }' | xargs -I {} scancel {}

docs.title: Delete all SLURM logs in the current directory (and all sub-directories)
docs.text: find . -type f -name "slurm-*.out" -user $USER -exec rm {} \;

docs.title: Re-assign the requested node
docs.text: squeue -h -u delhomme | awk '{if($5=="PD"){print $1}}' | xargs -I {} sudo scontrol update JobId={} ReqNodeList=picea

docs.title: Find all human-readble files with pattern in text
docs.text:
# Note the pattern that should be searched at the end of the command.
find . -type f -exec sh -c 'file -b {} | grep text &>/dev/null' \; -print | xargs -I {} grep -H "PATTERN" {}

docs.title: Counting all bp in a fasta file
docs.text: grep -v ">" CCS_FrameDP.fa | sed ':a;N;$!ba;s/\n//g' | wc

docs.title: Finding the intron size range
docs.text: grep intron TAIR10_GFF3_genes_transposons_introns.gff | awk 'BEGIN{max=0;min=0}{siz=$5-$4; if(siz>max){max=siz}; if(siz<min){min=siz}} END {print min+1 "-" max+1}'

docs.title: Getting the number of unmapped reads from STAR
docs.text:
In a star directory or by changing the find command to point to a star directory, it counts the number of unmapped reads (no matter why) per sample.

for f in `find . -name *final.out`; do echo `basename ${f//Log.final.out/}`; grep umber $f | grep reads | cut -f2 | awk '{if(NR==1){tot=$1}else{tot-=$1}}END{print tot}'; done

docs.title: convert sam to bam
docs.text: find . -name "*.sam" | xargs -I {} srun bash -c 'samtools view -b $0 | samtools sort - $0' {}

docs.title: Finding files longer than 80bp
docs.text: awk 'length > 80 {print FILENAME "(" FNR "): " $0}'

docs.title: Replacing TAB with 4 spaces
docs.text: find DESCRIPTION NAMESPACE NEWS vignettes/RnaSeqTutorial.Rnw man/*.Rd R/*.R | xargs -I {} bash -c 'perl -p -e "s/\t/    /g" $0 > $0.mod' {}

docs.title: Clean all stopped containers:
docs.text: docker rm $(docker ps -a -f "status=exited" -q)

docs.title: Clean all dangling images:
docs.text: docker rmi $(docker images -f "dangling=true" -q)

docs.title: Count the number of reads after the pipeline run
docs.text:
cd fastqc
find raw/multiview/*_1_fastqc -name fastqc_data.txt | sort | xargs -I {} bash -c 'echo $0 | awk -F_ "{printf \"%s_%s \",\$4,\$5}" ; grep "Total Sequences" $0 | awk "{print \$3}"' {}
